p_question-4676.ja:
以下は、留学生の作文である。


仲間からのメッセージ

ブイ フゥ テユエン

　日本に来てから 2 年間、私は大学の近くのレストランでアルバイトをしていました。店の人はみんな親切で、仕事で使う日本語がわからなくて私が困っていると、いつも優しく教えてくれました。大変なときもありましたが、みんなが助けてくれたので、続けることができました。

　[19]今学期から勉強が忙しくなって、先月アルバイトを辞めました。アルバイトの最終日に、店長から大きなカードをもらいました。店長は、店の仲間全員がメッセージを書いたと教えてくれました。カードに [20]のは、たくさんの感謝の言葉や応援の言葉でした。私はうれしくて、カードのメッセージを読みながら泣いてしまいました。

　もらったカードは部屋に飾って、 [21]に行く前に必ず見ています。頑張って働いた 2年間が目に見えるものになった感じがして、カードを見ると、元気が出ます。どんなに勉強が大変になっても、 [22]

The following is an essay by an international student.


Messages from My Coworkers

ブイ フゥ テユエン

　For the two years since coming to Japan, I worked part-time at a restaurant near my university. Everyone at the restaurant was kind, and when I was in trouble because I didn’t understand the Japanese used at work, they always taught me gently. There were tough times, but thanks to everyone’s help, I was able to keep going.

　[19] Since this semester my studies have become busy, I quit my part-time job last month. On my last day, I received a big card from the manager. The manager told me that everyone at the restaurant had written a message. What [20] on the card were many words of thanks and encouragement. I was so happy that I cried while reading the messages on the card.

　I have displayed the card I received in my room, and I always look at it before going [21]. It feels like the two years I worked hard have become something I can see, and when I look at the card, I feel energized. No matter how difficult my studies become, [22]

p_question-4680.ja:
（１）

　これは、あるホテルにチェックインした人が、フロントでもらったちらしである。


　8 月 11 日・12 日にホテル岩中にお泊まりのお客様

　「岩中村夏祭り」のご案内

　8 月 11 日(土)・12 日(日)に、岩中公園で夏祭りが行われます。

　会場とホテルの間は、ホテルのバスのご利用が可能です (約 5 分)。

　浴衣を着ている方は、会場内のどの店でもドリンクが 1 杯無料になります。皆様も、浴 衣を着て出かけてみてはいかがでしようか。

　ホテルでは、お好きな浴衣と帯を特別料金【1 日 2,网円】でお貸しします(浴衣の着方 がわからない方は、お手伝いいたします)。

　ご希望の方は、フロントでお申し込みください。

　ホテル岩中

　This is a flyer that someone received at the front desk when checking in to a hotel.

　Information on the “Iwanaka Village Summer Festival”

　The summer festival will be held at Iwanaka Park on Saturday, August 11 and Sunday, August 12.

p_question-4681.ja:
（２）

　先月、留学していたアメリカから帰国した。そして、知り合いに頼まれ、4 月から大学で数学を教えるこ とになった。働き始める前に、実家の部屋を整理しようと思って掃除をしていたら、小学生のときに書いた 「私の夢」という作文が出てきた。そこには、先生になりたいと書いてあった。そんなことはすっかり忘れて いたが、小さいときにそう書いていたのだ。とても驚いたが、子供のころの夢が本当になったのだし、頑張ろうと思う。

(2)

　Last month I returned from the United States, where I had been studying abroad. Then, at the request of an acquaintance, I was set to teach mathematics at a university starting in April. Before starting work, I decided to tidy up my room at my parents’ house, and while cleaning I found an essay I had written in elementary school titled “My Dream.” In it I had written that I wanted to become a teacher. I had completely forgotten that, but it seems I wrote so when I was little. I was very surprised, but since my childhood dream has come true, I think I will do my best.

p_question-4682.ja:
（３）

これはある日本語学校から学生に届いたメールである。


学校がもらった招待券の試合は今週末です。

日時: ２月 27 日(土) 開場 13 時 試合開始 14 時

場所: 春山公園野球場

まだ招待券を受け取っていない人は、２６日(金)までに事務室に取りに来てください。

自分だけで行くのが不安だと言う人がいたので、中川先生が一緒に行くことになりました。

一緒に行きたい人は、12 時に学校に集合してください。その場合、招待券は当日、中川先生からもらってもいいです。

自分で行く人は、学校にも野球場にも集合する必要はありません。

寒いですから、暖かい服装で行きましよう。

川野日本語学校

(3)

This is an email sent from a Japanese language school to its students.


The game for which the school received complimentary tickets is this weekend.

Date & Time: Saturday, February 27 Doors open 13:00 / Game starts 14:00

Location: Haruyama Park Baseball Stadium

If you have not yet picked up your complimentary ticket, please come to the office by Friday the 26th.

Since someone said they were nervous about going alone, Mr. Nakagawa will go together with students.

If you want to go together, please meet at the school at 12:00. In that case, you may receive your ticket from Mr. Nakagawa on the day.

If you are going on your own, you do not need to meet at the school or at the stadium.

It will be cold, so please come dressed warmly.

Kawano Japanese Language School

p_question-4683.ja:
（４）

　面白い実験を行った研究者がいる。その実験では、ある魚の顔に汚れをつけて鏡を見せた。すると、その魚は砂で汚れを落とすような動きをしてから、また鏡を見た。これは、自分の顔についた汚れを落とし、その後、汚れが消えたかどうかを確認する動作なのだそうだ。鏡の中にいる自分を見て、それが自分自身だとわかるのは、サルなどの頭のいい動物だけだと考えられていた。しかし、魚にもそれができるものがいるのだと、この研究者は言っている。

(4)

　There is a researcher who conducted an interesting experiment. In the experiment, they put a mark of dirt on a certain fish’s face and showed it a mirror. The fish then made movements as if rubbing off the dirt with sand and looked in the mirror again. They say this is the action of removing the dirt on its own face and then checking whether the dirt is gone. It had been thought that recognizing the self in a mirror—seeing the one in the mirror and understanding it is oneself—was something only intelligent animals like monkeys could do. However, this researcher says there are fish that can do it too.

p_question-4684.ja:
（１）

　最近、私と同じ 70 歳の友人が、自宅の車庫の壁に車をぶつけてしまったそうだ。私は運転に自信があったが、その話を聞いて、安全に運転が続けられるか不安になってきた。それで、①連転をやめることにして、車を売った。

　ただ、一つ②困ったことがあった。私は妻と二人で丘の上の家に住んでいる。毎日の買い物はこれまでと変わらず、二人で坂の下のスーパーまで行く。初めは、運動にもなるし、スーパーまで歩けばいいと考えていた。しかし、車を使わず荷物を持って板を上がって帰るのは、思ったより大変だったのだ。

　引っ越そうかと話すようになったとき、近所にスーパーの車がとまっているのを見た。インターネットで注文すると、店の商品は何でも届けてくれるそうだ。直接見て選べないので不安もあったが、まずは、肉と野葉を注文してみた。

　翌日届いた品物はどれもとても良かった。配達料はかかるが、便利さのほうが重要だ。いい方法が見つかった。これで引っ越しをしなくてもよさそうである。

(1)

　Recently, a friend the same age as me—70—apparently bumped his car into the wall of his home garage. I had been confident in my driving, but after hearing that, I began to worry about whether I could continue to drive safely. So I ① decided to stop driving and sold my car.

　However, there was one ② problem. My wife and I live together in a house on top of a hill. Our daily shopping was the same as before: the two of us went to the supermarket at the bottom of the slope. At first, I thought it would be good exercise to walk to the supermarket. However, without using a car, carrying our purchases and walking back up the hill was harder than I expected.

　When we had started talking about moving, I saw a supermarket van parked in the neighborhood. If you order on the internet, they will deliver anything the store sells. I was uneasy because you can’t look at the items directly to choose, but we tried ordering meat and vegetables first.

　Everything that arrived the next day was very good. There is a delivery fee, but the convenience is more important. We found a good solution. It looks like we won’t need to move.

p_question-4687.ja:
（２）

　日本では、音楽を聞きに行ったり、絵や映画を見に行ったりすることが好きな人でも、子供が小さいうちは、①行くのを我慢してしまうことがあります。子供は、騒いでしまう心配があるので連れて行けないし、人に預けることも簡単ではないからです。しかし、最近は少しずつですが、子供を頂かってくれる音楽会場や美術館、映画館などが増えてきました。また、②親子が楽しめるように工夫をするところも出てきています。例えば、ある映画館では、映面の間も会場を少し明るくしておくそうです。暗いと、怖がって泣いてしまう子供がいるからです。大きな音を聞くと、びつくりする子供もいるので、音も大きすぎないようにしてあります。

　このように、最近は、小さな子供がいる人が安心して、出かけて楽しめる場所が増えてきています。

(2)

　In Japan, even people who like going to listen to music or to see art or movies sometimes ① refrain from going while their children are small. That’s because you can’t take them for fear they might get noisy, and it isn’t easy to leave them with someone else. Recently, however, little by little, more concert halls, art museums, and movie theaters are accepting children. In addition, some places have begun ② making arrangements so that parents and children can enjoy themselves. For example, at one movie theater, they keep the auditorium slightly bright even during the film, because some children get scared and cry when it’s dark. Since some children are startled by loud sounds, they also keep the volume from being too loud.

　In this way, recently there are more and more places where people with small children can go out and have fun with peace of mind.

p_question-4690.ja:
1 年に２万通も感謝の手紙がくる会社がある。

　四国にある①靴の会社だ。初めはスリッパを作っていたのだが、約年前、お年寄りのための介護施設(注)の経営者から、こんな相談をされた。

　「施設内でお年寄りがよく転ぶので、困っているんです。いろいろ工夫しても解決できません。靴に問題がありそうなんです。」

　それて、お年寄りのための安全な靴を作ることにしたそうだ。社長はまず、約５００人のお年寄りの足を観察して、歩き方を調査することにした。そうして作った靴を履いてもらい、意見を聞きながら悪いところを直していった。２年後、「お年寄りのための靴」が完成したが、初めはあまり売れなかった。

　そこで、②調査結呆をもう一度確認することにした。すると、あることがわかった。お年寄りの中には年をとって片方の足だけが悪くなったり、足の形が変わってしまい左右の足の大きさが違ってきたりして、困っている人が少なくない。左右のセットではなく、片方の靴だけが必要な人もいるのだ。そこで、「お年奇りのための靴」を片方でも買えるようにした。

　靴の会社を経営しているある先輩からは、③そんなことをしたら経営がうまくいかなくなると反対されたが、社長はこの新しい販売方法を実行した。困っている人の問題を解決するためにいいものを作り、今までの靴の売り方とは全く違った売り方で、それが必要な人のところに届けようとしたのである。

　今では、この靴は１年に１８万足以上も売れている。2 万通も感謝の手紙がくるのは、④このような会社の姿勢があるからではないだろうか。

　(注) お年寄りのための介護施設: お年寄りが世話を受けながら生活する場所

There is a company that receives as many as 20,000 letters of appreciation in a year.

　It is a ①shoe company in Shikoku. At first they made slippers, but about some years ago, the operator of a nursing care facility (note) for the elderly consulted them as follows.

　“Elderly people often fall inside the facility, and we’re having trouble. We’ve tried various ideas but can’t solve it. It seems the problem may be with their shoes.”

　So they decided to make safe shoes for the elderly. The president first decided to observe the feet of about 500 elderly people and study how they walk. They had people wear the shoes made that way and, while listening to opinions, fixed the bad points. Two years later, the “shoes for the elderly” were completed, but at first they did not sell very well.

　Therefore, they decided to ②recheck the survey results. Then they discovered something. Not a few elderly people find that as they age, only one foot becomes weak, or the shape of the feet changes so that the sizes of the left and right feet become different, which causes problems. Some people need only one shoe instead of a pair. So they made it possible to buy the “shoes for the elderly” one at a time.

　A senior who runs a shoe company opposed the idea, saying that if they did ③such a thing, the business would not go well, but the president implemented this new sales method. To solve the problems of those in trouble, they made good products and, with a sales method completely different from how shoes had been sold before, tried to deliver them to the people who needed them.

　Now, more than 180,000 pairs of these shoes are sold in a year. The reason they receive as many as 20,000 letters of appreciation is probably because of the attitude of ④a company like this.

　(Note) Nursing care facility for the elderly: a place where elderly people live while receiving care

questions.stem: 毎日復習したので、よくできるようになった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: ふくしゅう is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"毎日復習したので、よくできるようになった。"  
→ "I was able to improve because I reviewed every day."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "復習" is read as **ふくしゅう**, which means *review* or *revision*. This is the correct reading because it refers to the act of going over material again to understand it better, as used in the context of the sentence.

**Other Options**  
1. ふうしゅう – Not a standard reading for 復習.  
2. れんしゅう – Means *practice* (kanji: 練習), which does not fit here.  
4. れいしゅう – Not a standard reading for 復習 or any typical kanji combination.

Only **ふくしゅう** matches the kanji **復習** in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「復習(ふくしゅう)」 — 복습. 「毎日復習する」와 어울린다.
오답: 1 ふうしゅう·4 れいしゅう는 없는 음. 2 れんしゅう는 「練習」(연습)의 음으로 헷갈리기 쉬운 함정.
포인트: 復=フク, 習=シュウ.

questions.stem: 小型のラジオが欲しいです。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: こがた is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"小型のラジオが欲しいです。"  
→ "I want a small-sized radio."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "小型" is read as **こがた**, which means *small-sized* or *compact*. This is the correct reading for the kanji in this context, describing the desired size of the radio.

**Other Options**  
1. こかた – Not a standard reading of 小型.  
2. しょうがた – Used in other contexts, but incorrect for 小型.  
4. しょうかた – Not a standard reading of 小型.

Only **こがた** matches the kanji **小型** in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「小型(こがた)」 — 소형. 「小型のラジオ」와 어울린다.
오답: 1 こかた·4 しょうかた는 없는 음. 2 しょうがた도 일반적이지 않음. 型가 음독(けい)이 아닌 훈독(かた)으로 읽힘.
포인트: 小型·大型(おおがた)는 훈독으로 「がた」.

questions.stem: 理由もないのに、人を疑ってはいけない。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: うたがって is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"理由もないのに、人を疑ってはいけない。"  
→ "You must not suspect people without a reason."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "疑って" is read as **うたがって**, which means *to suspect* or *to doubt*. This reading is appropriate for the kanji in this sentence.

**Other Options**  
1. きらって – Means *to dislike* (kanji: 嫌って), not applicable here.  
2. こわがって – Means *to be afraid* (kanji: 怖がって), does not fit the context.  
3. しかって – Means *to scold* (kanji: 叱って), unrelated to suspicion.

Only **うたがって** matches the kanji **疑って** in the given context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「疑(うたが)って」 — 의심하다. 「人を疑う」와 어울린다.
오답: 1 きらって는 「嫌って」, 2 こわがって는 「怖がって」, 3 しかって는 「叱って」 — 의미는 비슷한 부정 감정이나 한자가 다름.
포인트: 疑う=うたがう.

questions.stem: ここから先は道が細くなっている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: ほそく is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"ここから先は道が細くなっている。"  
→ "The road becomes narrow from here on."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "細く" is read as **ほそく**, which means *narrow* or *thin*. This is the correct reading for the context describing the narrowing of a road.

**Other Options**  
1. ひろく – Means *wide* (kanji: 広く), and does not match 細く.  
2. ふとく – Means *thick* (kanji: 太く), and is incorrect here as it implies the opposite.  
3. せまく – Means *narrow* (kanji: 狭く), which is similar in meaning but not the correct reading of 細く.

Only **ほそく** matches the kanji **細く** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「細(ほそ)く」 — 가늘게/좁게. 「道が細くなる」와 어울린다.
오답: 1 ひろく는 「広く」(넓게)로 반대 의미. 2 ふとく는 「太く」(굵게)로 반대. 3 せまく는 「狭く」(좁게)로 의미가 비슷하나 한자가 다름.
포인트: 細=ほそい/こまかい.

questions.stem: 川井さんは明日、退院するそうです。
questions.expl: **Correct Answer** Answer 1: たいいん is correct. **Question Translation** "川井さんは明日、退院するそうです。" → "It seems that Mr./Ms. Kawai will be discharged from the hospital tomorrow." **Explanation** The kanji "退院" is read as **たいいん**, which means *discharge from a hospital*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence. **Other Options** 2. たんいん – Not a standard reading of 退院. 3. だいいん – Not a standard reading of 退院. 4. だんいん – Not a standard reading of 退院. Only **たいいん** correctly matches the kanji **退院** for this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「退院(たいいん)」 — 퇴원. 「明日、退院する」와 어울린다.
오답: 2 たんいん·3 だいいん·4 だんいん은 없는 음. 退의 음독은 タイ.
포인트: 退=タイ, 院=イン. 반대어 入院(にゅういん).

questions.stem: パーティーには夫婦で参加します。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: ふうふ is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"パーティーには夫婦で参加します。"  
→ "We will attend the party as a couple."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "夫婦" is read as **ふうふ**, which means *husband and wife* or *married couple*. This is the correct reading as it accurately reflects the meaning in the sentence.

**Other Options**  
1. ふうふう – Not a standard reading of 夫婦.  
3. ふふう – Does not match any standard reading for 夫婦.  
4. ふふ – Not a correct reading for 夫婦.

Only **ふうふ** accurately reflects the kanji **夫婦** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「夫婦(ふうふ)」 — 부부. 「夫婦で参加する」와 어울린다.
오답: 1 ふうふう·3 ふふう·4 ふふ는 없는 음. 夫는 장음(ふう), 婦는 단음(ふ)인 점에 주의.
포인트: 夫=フウ, 婦=フ.

questions.stem: 留守の間に誰か来たようだ。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: るす is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"留守の間に誰か来たようだ。"  
→ "It seems someone came while I was away."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "留守" is read as **るす**, which means *being away from home* or *absence*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence.

**Other Options**  
1. るしゅ – Not a standard reading of 留守.  
3. りゅうす – Not a recognized reading of 留守.  
4. りゅうしゅ – Incorrect reading and does not match the context of 留守.

Only **るす** matches the kanji **留守** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「留守(るす)」 — 부재중. 「留守の間に」와 어울린다.
오답: 1 るしゅ·3 りゅうす·4 りゅうしゅ는 없는 음. 留는 보통 リュウ로 읽지만 「留守」는 특수 음독 ル.
포인트: 留守=るす (관용 음독).

questions.stem: これは、とても高価な花瓶だそうです。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: こうか is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"これは、とても高価な花瓶だそうです。"  
→ "They say this is a very expensive vase."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "高価" is read as **こうか**, which means *expensive* or *high-priced*. This reading fits the kanji given in the sentence context.

**Other Options**  
2. ごうか – Means *gorgeous* or *splendid* (豪華), and does not match 高価.  
3. こうが – Not a standard reading of 高価.  
4. ごうが – Also not a standard reading of 高価.  

Only **こうか** correctly represents the kanji **高価** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:

포인트: 高=コウ, 価=カ.

questions.stem: 彼はたんきだ。

→ "He is short-tempered."

The word "たんき" means *short-tempered*, and the correct kanji representation is **短気**. It describes someone who easily loses their temper or is quick to anger.

**Other Options**  
1. 男気（おとこぎ）– The reading of this kanji is "おとこぎ", and it means *manliness* or *chivalry*. It does not match the meaning of "たんき".

2. 単気（たんき）– While this has the correct reading, no such word exists with this kanji combination, making it irrelevant.

4. 断気（だんき）– This is read as "だんき", which means *cutting off* or *blocking air*, unrelated to being short-tempered.

Only **短気** accurately represents the word "たんき" in this context.
questions.expl_ko:

questions.stem: 上田さんは少ししんぱいしているようだ。

**Other Options**

3. 信配（しんぱい）– While this has the correct reading, this is not a standard kanji combination in Japanese, thus it doesn’t convey the meaning of *worry*.

4. 信酔（しんすい）– This is read as "しんすい", meaning *faith intoxication*, which isn’t a common phrase or related to *worry*.

Only **心配** accurately represents the word "しんぱい" in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:

questions.stem: 次はこれをやいてください。

"次はこれをやいてください。"  
→ "Next, please grill this."

**Explanation**  
The word "やいて" in this context means *to grill* or *to bake*, and the correct kanji representation is **焼いて**. It is used for cooking processes involving heat, such as grilling or toasting.

**Other Options**  
2. 燃いて（もえて）– The reading of this kanji is "もえて", which means *to burn*. It doesn’t match the cooking-related context of "やいて".

3. 煙いて（けむいて）– The reading is "けむいて", which means *to smoke* or *to be smoky*. This is unrelated to the intended action of cooking.

4. 炊いて（たいて）– This is read as "たいて", meaning *to cook (rice)* or *to boil*. It is not suitable for the context of grilling or baking.

Only **焼いて** correctly represents the word "やいて" when referring to grilling or baking in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「焼(や)いて」 — 굽다. 「これを焼いてください」와 어울린다.

questions.stem: 明日、かならず来てください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 必ず is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"明日、かならず来てください。"  
→ "Please make sure to come tomorrow."

The word "かならず" means *certainly* or *without fail*, and the proper kanji representation is **必ず**. It indicates that something will definitely happen or that someone should make sure to do something.

**Other Options**  
1. 確ず（たしかず）– Although "確" can relate to *certainty*, it's not the correct kanji for "かならず". "確か" (たしか) means *certain* but is used differently.

2. 要ず（ようず）– The reading "ようず" is incorrect, and "要" typically means *need* or *important*. It doesn't match the sound or meaning of "かならず".

4. 直ず（なおず）– This kanji is usually read as "なお" and means *straight* or *to fix*. It doesn't relate to "かならず".

Only **必ず** accurately represents the word "かならず" in this context.
questions.expl_ko:

오답: 1 確ず·2 要ず·4 直ず는 존재하지 않는 표기.
포인트: 必ず=かならず(부사). 「必」는 음독 ヒツ, 훈독 かなら(ず).

questions.stem: 雨が降ってきましたが、私の家は近いので、傘がなくても（　　　）です。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: 平気 (へいき) is correct. It means "okay" or "unbothered."

**Question Translation**  
It started to rain, but my house is nearby, so it’s okay even without an umbrella.

**Explanation**  
In the sentence, the speaker is saying that despite the rain, they are not worried because their house is nearby. The word 平気 (へいき) describes a state of being unaffected or unconcerned, which fits the context of being okay without an umbrella because of the short distance to home.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: 安全 (あんぜん) means "safety." Although it generally reflects being secure, it does not fit properly in the context where the emphasis is more on being unfazed or unbothered by the rain, not on physical safety.

- Answer 3: 正常 (せいじょう) means "normal" or "normalcy." Using this here doesn't fit the context since the sentence is about showing that the rain isn't causing a problem or concern, rather than discussing what is normal.

- Answer 4: 十分 (じゅうぶん) means "enough" or "sufficient." This does not make sense in the given context as it cannot describe the state of being unbothered by the rain.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The phrase "平気でいる" is often used to express a state of not being worried or bothered by something, even when faced with potentially inconvenient situations. Recognizing expressions like 平気 can help convey a calm or unaffected demeanor in various contexts.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「平気(へいき)」 — 괜찮음, 아무렇지도 않음. 「傘がなくても平気だ」와 어울린다.
오답: 2 安全(안전)은 위험 회피, 3 正常(정상)은 상태가 정상, 4 十分(충분)은 양적 충분으로 문맥에 어긋남.
포인트: 平気だ=괜찮다, 끄떡없다.

questions.stem: 仕事で疲れているときは、夕飯を作るのが（　　　）ので、外食することが多い。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: めんどうくさい is correct. It means "bothersome" or "troublesome."

**Question Translation**  
When I'm tired from work, making dinner is bothersome, so I often eat out.

**Explanation**  
In the context of the sentence, the speaker is describing a situation where they are exhausted from work and thus find cooking dinner to be a hassle. The term めんどうくさい aptly describes something inconvenient or laborious, aligning perfectly with the context in which the person prefers the convenience of eating out over cooking.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: だらしない means "sloppy" or "untidy." It generally describes a person’s appearance or habits and does not fit the context of finding an activity troublesome.
- Answer 3: くわしい means "detailed" or "well-informed." It doesn't make sense in this context as it doesn't relate to the task of cooking being a hassle.
- Answer 4: にくらしい means "hateful" or "detestable." This doesn't apply here, as it conveys a stronger emotional dislike not typically used to describe mundane tasks like cooking.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The expression めんどうくさい (面倒くさい) is commonly used in Japanese to express that something feels like too much effort or is bothersome. Recognizing such expressions can aid in understanding sentiments related to daily activities or tasks.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「めんどうくさい」 — 귀찮다. 「夕飯を作るのがめんどうくさい」와 어울린다.
오답: 1 だらしない(흐트러져 있다), 3 くわしい(상세하다), 4 にくらしい(밉살스럽다)는 문맥에 맞지 않음.
포인트: 피곤할 때 + 외식 → 「귀찮다」가 자연스러움.

questions.stem: ゆうべはほとんど寝ていないので、今日は眠くて何度も（　　　）が出てしまう。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: あくび is correct. It means "yawn."

**Question Translation**  
Since I didn't sleep much last night, I'm sleepy today and keep yawning repeatedly.

**Explanation**  
The context of the sentence indicates that the speaker is feeling sleepy due to a lack of sleep from the night before. A yawn (あくび) is a common physical response to feeling tired or sleepy. Thus, あくび fits perfectly in the blank as it describes an action associated with sleepiness.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: しゃっくり (hiccup) refers to an involuntary spasm of the diaphragm, typically unrelated to tiredness or sleepiness. It doesn’t match the context of being something that happens repeatedly due to tiredness.
- Answer 2: くしゃみ (sneeze) often occurs due to irritation in the nose from allergies or illness, not sleepiness, so it doesn’t fit the context of sleep deprivation resulting in a repeated action.
- Answer 3: せき (cough), like sneezing, is associated with throat irritation or illness rather than lack of sleep, making it an unsuitable choice for describing a response to sleepiness.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
Understanding the context-related meaning of physical reactions such as あくび (yawn) is essential in comprehension. Japanese often expresses physical states or actions succinctly, so recognizing these specific terms allows quick identification of the correct expression.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「あくび」 — 하품. 「眠くてあくびが出る」와 어울린다.
오답: 1 しゃっくり(딸꾹질), 2 くしゃみ(재채기), 3 せき(기침) — 모두 생리현상이나 졸음과 직접 연관되는 것은 하품.
포인트: 眠い+出る → 「あくび」 콜로케이션.

questions.stem: 「私の国の社会問題」という（　　　）で、レポートを書くことになった。

questions.stem: この季節は、昼と夜の気温の（　　　）が大きい。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 差 (さ) is correct. It means "difference" or "gap."

**Question Translation**  
During this season, the difference in temperature between day and night is significant.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes a noticeable difference in temperatures between day and night. The word 差 (さ) is commonly used to refer to a "difference" or "gap" between two measures, such as temperature, height, or time. Thus, "気温の差" (きおんのさ) correctly indicates the "temperature difference," fitting perfectly in this context.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 台 (だい) means "platform" or "stand" and is associated with objects like vehicles or machines. It doesn’t convey any sense of difference or variation in temperature.
- Answer 2: 段 (だん) means "step" or "grade," often referring to levels or steps in a series. While it can imply stages or levels, it doesn’t fit naturally in describing temperature differences.
- Answer 4: 列 (れつ) means "row" or "line," used to depict things arranged in an order or sequence. It doesn’t relate to measuring or comparing temperature differences.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The term "差" (さ) is often used in contexts where a comparison or contrast between two things is involved, such as 温度差 (おんどさ, temperature difference) or 年齢差 (ねんれいさ, age difference). Recognizing words that imply comparison or distinction is crucial for understanding contextually-defined expressions in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「差(さ)」 — 차이. 「気温の差が大きい」와 어울린다.
오답: 1 台(받침/대), 2 段(단), 4 列(열)은 차이를 나타내지 못함.
포인트: 気温の差/温度差 — 「差」가 정답 콜로케이션.

questions.stem: （　　　）して、鍵をかけないで家を出てしまった。

**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: うっかり is correct. It means "carelessly" or "inadvertently."

**Question Translation**  
(I) inadvertently left the house without locking the door.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes an action where the speaker left the house without locking the door, implying it was due to forgetfulness or carelessness. "うっかり" (ukkari) is used to express actions done without careful thought, often leading to mistakes or oversights. It fits perfectly here as it emphasizes the unintentional nature of not locking the door.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: はっきり (hakkiri) means "clearly" or "distinctly." It doesn't fit the context as it doesn't relate to an unintended action or mistake.
- Answer 2: しっかり (shikkari) means "firmly" or "securely." This would indicate a deliberate action which is opposite to the accidental nature implied by the sentence.
- Answer 3: ぴったり (pittari) means "exactly" or "perfectly." It refers to a precise fit or match, which is not relevant in a context that describes a mistake or oversight.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
Understanding adverbs like うっかり that express an unintentional or careless action is crucial in Japanese. These words help convey the speaker's subjective feelings about their actions, often implying regret or oversight. This nuance is essential for determining the appropriate context in such expressions.
questions.expl_ko:

questions.stem: まだ空席が少しあるので、今なら予約は（　　　）です。
